heading: Limitations
body:
- *We strongly recommend that you proactively monitor your list of non-compliant resources*
  using the [/orgs/\{ORG-ID}/nonCompliantResources](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-getorgnoncompliantresources) endpoint.
  Atlas Resource Policies ensure compliance with defined goals but do not enforce intermediate
  or transitional states. For example, if a downstream component fails, the policy
  may temporarily enter a non-compliant intermediate state.

- Each Atlas Resource Policy must have a unique name. If you attempt to create an Atlas Resource Policy with
  an existing name, the server returns a `400 (Bad Request)` status code.

- It's possible to create identical Atlas Resource Policies with different names. While this doesn't
  cause functional conflicts, it can lead to redundancy and confusion in policy management.

- Atlas Resource Policies are allow-by-default. If no Atlas Resource Policies exist, users can perform all actions on Atlas clusters
  that their [Atlas User Roles](user-roles) allows.

- Our implemented version of [Cedar policy language](https://docs.cedarpolicy.com/) supports only one Cedar policy per `.cedar` file, but you
  can list multiple Cedar policies (`.cedar` files) in your Atlas Resource Policy.
  Each `.cedar` file is identified in the [Atlas Administration API](atlas-admin-api)
  response as a unique 24-hexadecimal character string after `policies.id`.

- If the [Cluster Auto-Scaling](cluster-autoscaling) limits on your existing cluster
  don't match the new Atlas Resource Policy, the cluster might exceed the policy's defined boundaries.
  To address this issue, use the [/orgs/\{ORG-ID}/nonCompliantResources](api/doc/atlas-admin-api-v2/operation/operation-/orgs/\{ORG-ID}/nonCompliantResources)
  endpoint to identify and monitor non-compliant resources so you can update them to meet
  policy requirements.

- Before enabling network-layer controls, review your existing [IP Access List](access-list)
  to ensure it contains all necessary access points.

- Private endpoint definitions in [Cedar policy language](https://docs.cedarpolicy.com/) require that you enter individual IP addresses,
  as wildcard operations are not supported.

- When enforcing disk size, ensure you account for the specific requirements of your
  instance sizes and cloud provider limits.

- If you [Enforce Minimum Shard Count](restrict-shard-count) greater than one,
  you must explicitly allow the transition state for cluster conversions in your policy
  (for example, `&& !context.cluster.isConvertingToSharded`).
  Without this exception, Atlas blocks users from converting a replica set to
  a sharded cluster because the conversion process requires an intermediate state
  where the cluster has only one shard.

- Atlas treats a replica set as having a shard count of one. If you enforce a
  minimum shard count greater than one without checking whether clusters are sharded clusters,
  all replica sets in the project are marked as non-compliant.
